title: Gel wax jar candle
subtitle: A transparent mineral gel candle poured into a jar with optional decorative embeds
excerpt: Gel wax is entirely different from any other candle wax. It is mineral-based, crystal clear, and poured at a much higher temperature than soy or beeswax. The result is a glass-like candle that can showcase embeds suspended inside it. Bubbles are the enemy; high pour temperature and a slow, steady hand prevent them.
sourceNotes: Gel wax candle technique from CandleScience and standard candle-making references. Gel wax properties from mineral gel wax datasheets.

recipe.batchNotes: Multiply quantities per jar. Work quickly as gel wax sets faster than soy wax once below 70 degrees Celsius.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best made shortly before gifting or use. Embeds can be prepared days ahead.
recipe.temperatureNote: Melt gel wax and pour at 90 to 95 degrees Celsius to prevent air bubbles. Add embeds when wax has cooled to 80 degrees Celsius.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 2 years. The fragrance oil may diminish over time. Discard if the gel discolours or the fragrance becomes unpleasant.

recipeTools.notes: Gel wax reaches 95 degrees Celsius; use glass or stainless steel
Essential; gel wax must be poured at a precise temperature
Pre-warmed in the oven at 100 degrees Celsius for 10 minutes to prevent cracking

glossaryTerms.definition: A transparent, mineral oil-based wax with a jelly-like texture. It has a high melting point (around 85 to 90 degrees Celsius for medium-density gel), holds fragrance well, and must be poured at high temperature to avoid cloudiness and air bubbles.

A decorative object suspended inside a gel wax candle. Embeds must be non-flammable, heat-safe, and positioned outside the wick's melt pool burn path. Sea glass, shells, and glass beads are popular choices.

A candle wick with a zinc metal core. Used in gel wax candles because the rigid core holds the wick upright in the liquid gel, and the zinc does not discolour the transparent wax as a cotton wick can.
The percentage of fragrance oil relative to wax weight. Gel wax holds 6% fragrance; exceeding this causes the fragrance oil to pool at the bottom as the gel cannot absorb more.
The strength and reach of a fragrance from a burning candle.

items.prepNote: To fill a 250 ml jar, allowing for shrinkage
6% of wax weight; must be non-polar (check gel wax compatibility with supplier)
Sea glass, shells, or glass beads; must be non-flammable

content.text: Pre-warm the glass jar in an oven at 100 degrees Celsius for 10 minutes, then remove and place on a heat-safe surface. This prevents the hot wax from cracking cold glass. Secure the zinc wick in the centre of the jar using a wick centring tool or two pencils across the rim.

Melt the gel wax in a double boiler, stirring gently and avoiding vigorous mixing which creates bubbles. Heat to 95 degrees Celsius. Add the fragrance oil at 90 degrees Celsius and stir slowly. Pour the gel into the warm jar in a slow, steady stream to minimise bubble introduction.

If adding embeds, wait until the gel cools to 80 degrees Celsius and gently push embeds into position with a wooden skewer. Position them outside the approximate 3 cm radius around the wick where the melt pool will form. Leave to set undisturbed for at least 4 hours.
Gel candles keep for 2 years. Store upright in a cool, dark spot. Do not burn for more than 4 hours at a time. Trim the wick to 5 mm before each burn. Never leave a burning candle unattended.
